Miniax M2.7 new free self-improving Chinese AI. So an AI just helped build a better version of itself. Not a little better, not a small update, a version that is measurably smarter, measurably faster, measurably more useful. And that AI did somewhere between 30 and 50% of the work it took to create it. Let that sink in for one second. The engineers didn't just build this thing. They handed a big chunk of the AI job to the AI itself. The AI did it over and over. 100 rounds of testing, fixing, rewriting, improving automatically without being asked each time. This is Miniaax M 2.7. It dropped on March the 18th, 2026, just yesterday. And I want to walk you through exactly what happened, why it matters, and exactly what you can do with it today for free, right? Because here's the thing that I keep coming back to. This isn't a story about one cool AI model. This is a story about a turning point. A moment where AI stopped being just a tool you use and started becoming a tool that actually improves itself. And once you understand that, once it actually clicks for you, the question changes. It's no longer should I learn to use AI. The question becomes, how fast can I get up to speed before the people around me are laps ahead. But let's start with the story because the story here is wild. There's a company in Shanghai called Miniax. Most people in the west have never heard of them. That's not an accident. They've been building quietly, very quietly. Whilst everyone was talking about Chachi BT and Claude and Gemini, Miniax was actually doing something very different. Their stated mission is intelligence with everyone. That's not marketing fluff. That's actually how they've they built models that could compete with the best in the world and made them available um at a fraction of the cost of American alternatives. But the M2.7 release changes the conversation entirely. Miniax has built M2.7 to build, monitor, and optimize its own reinforcement learning harnesses. This move towards recursive self-improvement signals a shift in the industry. Recursive self-improvement, that's a fancy phrase. Uh, let me tell you what that actually means in English. Imagine you're trying to get better at baking bread. Normally, you bake a loaf, taste it, think that wasn't quite right. Adjust the recipe yourself and try again. You're doing all the learning. You're doing all the adjusting yourself. Now imagine the oven itself tasted the bread, reviewed the whole recipe, figured out what went wrong, rewrote the instructions, and started the next batch automatically. That's roughly what's happening here. When developing M2.7, Miniax let the model update its own memory and build dozens of complex skills in its harness to help with reinforcement learning experiences. They further let the model improve its own learning process and harness based on the experiment results. The AI was not just running tasks. It was redesigning the process it used to run tasks. It was writing better instructions for itself. It was finding patterns in its own failures and fixing them. And here's the number that stop me cold. The model can deeply participate in its own training and iterative optimization processes taking on about 30 to 50% of the workload in certain research and development scenarios. So between 1/3 and 1 half of the work to build M2.7 was done by the AI itself. A year ago, that sentence would have sounded like science fiction. Today, it's a press release. Now, I know what some of you are thinking right now. You're thinking, Julian, come on. This is what AI companies always say. They pull out a paper, they show some benchmarks, and they tell you it's revolutionary, and then it's exactly the same as last time. I hear you. I've covered enough of these releases to understand that skepticism, but this one is different for a very specific reason. I'm going to show you exactly why. After each iteration round, M2.7 generates a short-term memory file and simultaneously performs self-criticism on its current results, providing potential optimization directions for the next round. The next round then conducts further self-optim based on the memory and self feedback chain from all previous rounds. They ran this process for 100 rounds, not once, not five times, 100 rounds. And the result, they achieved a 30% improvement on performance on internal evaluation sets. The AI got 30% better at its own job by itself by reviewing what went wrong, making a plan, and trying again over and over. That's not a feature, that's a behavior. And that's what learning looks like. And then to really stress test this idea, Miniax did something even bolder. They had M2.7 participate in 22 machine learning competitions at the MLE Benchite level. Open sourced by OpenAI. These competitions can be run on a single GPU yet they cover virtually all stages of machine learning workflow. So they basically said okay AI go compete in 22 real machine learning tournaments against other approaches. See how you do. And the result was the best run achieved nine gold medals. Five silver gold medal uh sorry five silver medals and one bronze medal. The average medal rate across the three runs was 66.6%. Second only to Opus 4.6 75.7% GB 5.4 and 71.2%. 2% tying with Gemini 3.1 at 66.6%. So, let me repeat that. A Chinese AI company just released a model that tied with Google's Gemini 3.1 on competitive machine learning benchmarks by having the model compete autonomously in 22 tournaments. This is not a small company showing off anymore. This is a genuine competitor playing at the top table. Now, here's what we need to talk about in terms of what M2.7 can actually do. not in theory, in practice, because the self-improving story is genuinely exciting. But you're probably sitting there wondering, okay, what does that mean for me? If you're a business owner, if you're a freelancer, if you're someone just trying to figure out how AI fits into your work, what does any of this actually mean? So, let's talk about coding first because the coding numbers are stunning. On SWE Pro, which covers multiple programming languages, M2.7 achieved a 56.22% accuracy rate, matching GPT 5.3 codeex. It demonstrates an even more notable advantage on benchmarks closer to a real world engineering scenario such as S. S. S. S. S. S. S. S. S. S. SW Multilingual at 76.5 and MultiSswbench at 52.7. Now, if you're not a coder, let me tell you why this matters to you. SWE Pro is not a test where AI answers quiz questions about coding. It's a test where AI has to go into a real GitHub repository, a real codebase that real developers built, find the bug, write the fix, and make the test pass. It's the equivalent of dropping a new employee into a complex, messy project on day one with no training and saying, "Find the problem and fix it." A score of more than 50% indicates that Miniax M2.7 can handle real world engineering work. Um, not just theoretical code prompts. More than half the time, M2.7 can walk into a realworld codebase it's never seen before and fix a bug correctly. But it goes even further than just fixing bugs. This capability extends to endto-end full project delivery scenarios. On the Vibe Pro benchmark, M2.7 scored 55.6% nearly on par with Opus 4.6, meaning that whether the requirement involves web, Android, iOS, or simulation tasks, they can be handled and handed directly to M2.7 to compete end to end for project delivery. That phrase is doing a lot of work. So, let me tell you what it means. You describe the app you want. The AI builds the whole thing, not just a snippet, not just one file, the whole project working deployable. More than half the time, M2.7 can do that on its own. Now, I want you to think about what that means for everyone who's ever wanted to build a product but felt like they couldn't because they didn't know how to code. The wall is coming down. It's not fully down yet. It still takes skill to direct these tools, to know what you want, to check the output, to iterate. But the gap between I have an idea and I have a working product is shrinking at a pace that would have seemed impossible years ago. Let's talk about the office stuff because I know most of you watching this aren't software developers. You work in business. you deal with spreadsheets and presentations and reports and emails. And I don't want to gloss over what M2.7 can do in those areas. In the professional office software domain, the model's ELO score on GBT val AIA is 1495, the highest among open source models. M2.7 shows significantly improved ability for complex editing in the office suite, Excel, PowerPoint, and Word, and can better handle multi-and revisions and highfidelity edits. Highest among open- source models for office work. Let that land. And they stress tested this in a real specific way that I think a lot of people can relate to. The company highlighted M2.7's capabilities in a complex stress test involving Taiwan semiconductor manufacturing. M2.7 was tasked with reading annual reports, cool transcripts, cross referencing multiple research papers, and independently designing prediction models. The AI successfully generated a complete output package, including a SL a slide deck, a word-based research report, and Excel charts. Think about that again slowly. M2.7 read the annual reports. It cross referenced the research papers. It built the model. It made the PowerPoint. It wrote the word document. And it built the Excel charts. All of it from scratch. Industry feedback indicates the output quality is sufficient to serve as a first draft in professional workflows. Effectively compressing hours of human labor into minutes. Hours of human labor into minutes. That's not an exaggeration. I want to be careful not to upsell this. You still need a human to review it, to check numbers, to add judgment, add context. But the hours of data gathering, formatting, building, structuring, and um everything else, that work is disappearing. Think about what that means for a small business owner who's been putting off like doing proper analysis because it takes too long. Think about what it means for a freelancer who's been, you know, charging for hours of research and reporting or what that means for a team that's been hiring analysts to do the first draft work. All of that is getting automated. Now, I want to talk to you about the agent stuff, the multi- aent stuff, because this is where things get genuinely new. M 2.7 introduces native support for agent teams, clusters of AI agents that collaborate with distinct roles. Unlike previous iterations that relied on heavy prompt engineering, M2.7 creates strict role boundaries and protocols, allowing agents to challenge each other's logic and conduct adversarial reasoning to reduce errors. Now, let me explain what that means as an analogy. Imagine you're building a house. In the old way of AI, you had one worker and that one worker was very smart but could only focus on one thing at a time. You have to tell them each step. You have to check each step. It got the job done but is very slow. Now imagine a whole crew. One person does the framing. One person does the plumbing. One does the electrical. And here's the key part. They're actually arguing with each other. The plumber is saying, "Wait, if you put that wall there, I can't run my pipes." The electrician is saying, "Hold on. I need to see the plans for the framing before I commit." They're checking each other's work, catching each other's mistakes. That's what agent teams does. Multiple AI agents, each with a specific job, each reviewing the others, each catching the errors the others miss. M2.7 maintains a 97% skill adherence rate whilst working with over 40 complex skills simultaneously. 7% of the time when you give M2.7 more than 40 complex tools to work with simultaneously, it does exactly what it's supposed to do. That number matters a lot because one of the biggest problems with AI agents has always been that they go off the rails. You give them a task and they start doing something adjacent to the task or they get confused and loop or they make up information to fill in the gaps. We've all seen that with stuff like open claw before. M2.7 is dramatically more reliable at staying on track which means you can trust it with longer, more complex chains of work. I want to talk about something that the tech press isn't covering nearly enough. There's a live production story buried in these announcements that every business owner needs to hear. The model's ability to reduce recovery time for live production incidents to under 3 minutes by autonomously correlating monitoring metrics with code repositories suggests a paradigm shift for S sur and DevOps teams. Under 3 minutes to recover from a live production incident. If you run any kind of software, any kind of app, any kind of website, you know how catastrophic a production incident can be. Systems go down, customers leave, engineers scramble, it can take hours to find the root cause, fix it, and get back online. M2.7 is cutting that to under 3 minutes by connecting the monitoring data, reading the logs, finding the problem, and deploying the fix automatically. That's not future technology. It's happening right now. And companies are already deploying this. Think about what that means for any business. It runs on software. Think about the cost or the downtime, right? Think about the hours of engineering team time and teams that go into incident response. All of that or a huge chunk of it can be automated. And this is just one example, but the same underlying capability, AI that can read complex systems, understand what went wrong, and fix it quickly, applies to a thousand different business processes. Now, I want to talk about something that I think is bigger and even bigger than everything I've just described. Because what really got my attention with M2.7 release isn't the benchmark, it's what the benchmarks are pointing to. If models can effectively build better versions of themselves, then building like a next generation AI development could see a totally structural different shift, right? So let me translate that into plain English. Right now to make a better AI you need a huge team of research uh researchers. You need a lot of computer value. You need months of work. Each generation of AI is better than the last but it requires not a lot of resources to get there. What M2.7 is showing is a different path. What if the AI can do 30% or 40% or 50% of the work itself? What if a process of making smarter starts to require less human labor and less time with every generation? What happens to the speed of AI progress when AI is contributing to its own improvement? I keep saying this and people keep nodding, but I'm not really sure it's landing. We are not on a straight line of progress here. We're potentially entering a curve, a curve that bends upward faster than anyone is prepared for. So, this compounds, right? And the reason compounding is powerful isn't like the first few years. It's the late years when the compounding is compounding on itself, right? When the growth is feeding itself. AI that contributes to its own improvement is the beginning of compounding for intelligence. Miniax calls it early echoes of self-evolution. That's the official title of their blog post. Early echoes. They're not claiming to have some solved AI self-improvement. The same way at the beginning, the early echoes. And I believe them. And that's what scares me. Not in a bad way, but in the way that makes you realize you do need to pay attention. Now, let's talk about the open claw angle. Because if you've been watching this channel, you know I've talked about open a lot. And the connection between M2.7 and OpenClaw is a big deal. OpenClaw is an AI agent framework. It's a way to run AI agents on your computer or in the cloud that can do real world tasks. Browse the web, write and run code, create files, send messages, interact with other cell phones. Think of it as the environment where the AI does the actual work. And M2.7 was specifically designed to work inside environments like Open Claw. Minia's announcement describes M2.7 as their first model that deeply participates in their own evolution. available now in like for example kilo code. You can also use it inside zo computer. Early open claw users report superior multi-turn coding debugging and long context performance compared to m2.5. So real people using it right now in production are saying it's better than its predecessor in the exact tasks you'd use open core 4 multi-term coding debugging long context work right so for example this morning I actually built out a app which was a pomodoro type and it literally just launched itself and and created itself perfectly first time round. actually created another website that works perfectly first time around. So, it's really powerful and actually works in real life tests, not just on the benchmarks. In the mm claw evaluation, which tests an AI ability to use complex tools and implement long horizon tasks, M2.7 achieved a 62.7% accuracy rate, nearing the performance of Sonic 4.6. Now, if it's nearing the performance of Sonic 4.6 on agent task, bear in mind, this is a lot cheaper as a model, right? the API costs are much cheaper and you can even choose try it for free. Zo computer actually offering this for free with Miniaax and Kim K 2.5 free until the end of March. So I want to take a minute here because I think we need to step back and look at the bigger picture. What's happening with Miniax is not happening in isolation. Multi sorry Miniax becomes the second Chinese startup to release a proprietary cuttingedge large language model in recent months following Z AI with its GM 5 Turbo and there are rumors that Alibaba's Quent team is also launching something big soon too. There's a shift happening in Chinese AI. For the last few years a lot of the exciting Chinese AI models were open source, free to download, free to run, free to build on. Now those same companies are moving towards proprietary models and keeping the best stuff behind a payw wall but building real businesses. And that tells you something important. It tells you they're confident enough in the models to charge for them. It tells you the quality is there. It tells you the gap between Chinese AI and American AI is not as wide as a lot of people assumed. Miniax actually scored 50 on the artificial analysis intelligence index, representing a 8 point improvement over its predecessor in just one month and taking eighth place overall globally in terms of intelligence across benchmarking tests and tasks in various domains. And bear in mind, it's starting to self-improve. So what happens to the rankings next month? What happens when the self-improvement loop runs for another 30 days or another 60? This is not the ceiling. This is a flaw. Now let me tell you about a specific behavior that Kilo code, one of the developer tools, integrates with M2.7 that they noticed when they benchmarked this model. M2.7 consumes roughly 2.8 million input tokens per trial on average, which is on the higher end of any model tested. M2.7 reads more context per step than any other model, which means it accumulates more tokens over the course of a task. It's a really interesting deal. M2.7 reads more. It gathers more information before it acts. It's thorough in a way that other models aren't. When it works, that thoroughess produces correct solutions. When it doesn't, it means more tokens and time spent on each task. So, it's a model that does overexplore. It would rather gather more information and take longer than Russian get it wrong. So, it's a strong pick when you're working on tasks that reward deep context gathering. For example, like complex refactors, codebase wide changes or anything when understanding surrounding code matters more than speed. You want to think about what kind of tasks reward that behavior, right? Complex analysis, research, heavy work, documents where you need to cross reference a lot of information, modeling also those are the same tasks right now that are being done by, you know, expensive human professionals, analysts, research assistants, entry- level consultants. The task that reward thoroughess and information gathering over speed. M2.7 is specifically good at those tasks. Now, here is the part of the video where I need to be honest with you about something because I could just sit here and say AI is getting better. Everything is amazing. You should be excited. That would be a disservice to you. But here's what's also true. The world has always had jobs that required gathering information, reading documents, cross referencing cross referencing data, building first drafts, and writing reports. Those jobs have employed millions of people. They're good jobs, stable jobs, jobs that people built careers on. And what I'm describing today is a technology that's getting very good at doing exactly those things. Not perfectly, not without human oversight, but well enough to change the demand for those skills. And I don't say that to be scary. I say it because I think the honest thing to do is to look at what's happening and decide how you're going to respond. Because here's the truth. Every time technology has changed the nature of work, there have been people who figured out how to use the new technologies to do things that weren't possible before, to reach people they couldn't reach, to offer services they couldn't offer, to build things they couldn't build. For example, like the printing press, it didn't just get rid of jobs. It created an entire world of publishing, journalism, advertising, education that hadn't existed before. The internet didn't just automate retail. It created a global marketplace where someone in a small town in the Philippines could sell products to someone in London. So AI is not just a threat to certain kinds of work. It's an enormous amplifier for people who learn how to use it. But, and this is the part that I want to land hard, the amplification doesn't happen automatically. You have to learn the tools. You have to understand what they're good at. You have to build workflows that leverage them. the person who spent 2004 and 2025 figuring out how to use AI agents effectively, they're sitting on a completely different capability set than the person who spent the same time watching and waiting. And that gap is getting wider. I want to give you a very a few like specific examples of what people are doing right now with AI agents. So for example, this morning actually used it to automate a website that I built and another app that I built. It would be great for scheduling tasks as well. You can do that inside Zo computer with a combination of M2.7 as well. Dashboards, customer portals, automated workflows, things that would have been expensive to build with a development agency and now being built in days for almost nothing. So in every one of these cases, the person who went from one level to the other didn't happen to to get there by accident. They learned the tools. They experimented. They built new workflows. Now, I want to come back to the self-improvement story one more time because I don't think we fully reckoned what it actually means. Miniax put out a statement that I think is one of the most significant things any company has said publicly this year. They believe the future AI self-evolution will gradually transition towards full autonomy, coordinating data construction, model training, inference architecture, evaluation, and other stages throughout human involvement. They're not saying they're there. They're very clear that M2.7 is early echoes, but that's the direction and that's where this is going. And if that direction continues, even partially, the speed at which AI improves is going to be different from anything we've seen in the history of technology. But think about how long it took to go from like GPT2 to GPT3. About a year and a half. GPT3 to GPT4, another year and a half. Now think about what happens when the model is contributing to its own next version. When the loop gets tighter, when the feedback cycles run faster because the AI is running them instead of humans rather than relying solely on humanled finetuning, Miniax has built M2.7 to monitor and optimize its own reinforcement learning harnesses. The human research is still there, but they're moving from doing the work to overseeing the work, reviewing, making critical decisions, having discussions about direction. These could have required the collaboration of multihuman researchers from different teams before, but now human researchers only interact for critical decisions and discussions. This accelerates um problem discovery and experimentation, delivering models faster and faster. Again, the word that keeps showing up in every AI story this year is actually faster. Now, let's zoom out and look at where Miniax M2.7 sits in the global landscape because I think this is important context, too. M2.7 scored 86.2% on Pinchbench, landing just behind GLM5 and GPT 5.4 at 86.4 and just ahead of Quen 3.5 Plus at 85.8. Top five in the world on agent benchmarks. The Chinese model available on free tiers, right behind the very best from OpenAI. They jump from M2.5 at 82.5% to M2.7 at 86.2% 2% is a 3.7 point improvement that moved Miniax from the middle of the pack to into the top tier. This is a model released 1 month after its predecessor, right? And in just one month, it jumped from midpack to top tier. And what's wild is that this is happening at the same time that Chinese AI is shifting strategy. They're moving from open source toward proprietary. They're building real businesses. They're competing on quality. Minimax serves over 130,000 enterprise clients and millions of independent creators already across the globe, right? So this isn't just like a research project anymore. This is a company with real customers that's listing and and releasing models that compete with the best in the world. And the western AI industry, OpenI, Anthropic, Google, they're all aware of this, right? They're all watching this cuz competition is real right now, which is actually good for you and me cuz competition means better tools cheaper. Competition means open claw models getting faster. Competition means the barriers to accessing frontier AI continue to drop. But it also means the timeline is accelerating. If multiple labs are racing to self-improving AI, the curve bends up even faster. So, let me talk to you about a few things you can do today right now. You can access it from the Kilo dashboard. If you want to try an agent that can write code, run code, browse the web, manage files, and do complex multisep tasks. This is the setup to try. You don't need to mess around in terms. So if that sounds like you, go to Z computer and try it. The free window is closing. Now, here's the thing that I want to leave you with, right? Because I've covered the technology, I've covered the numbers, I've covered the benchmarks. I want to talk about the human side of this. So, I think there's an emotional component to AR progress that doesn't get talked about enough. A lot of people are scared. Not in an obvious way, not in the way they'd admit on a Zoom call, but quietly scared watching these releases, reading these stories, wondering where they fit. You know, for example, is my job going away. It's a thing that I've spent years getting good at suddenly worth less. Am I going to get left behind? I'm not going to sit here and tell you those fears are baseless. They're not. Some things are changing. Some skills that were valuable are becoming less val. That's real. But here's what I also know. The people I've seen thrive through every wave of technological change are not the people who had the most to lose. They're the people who move fast. Right? It's the copyriter who learned to use AI tools in 2023. You know, the copyriter who learned to use AI tools in 23 is not competing with AI. They're a creative director who produces 10 times more with a small team of AI tools. They know how to direct. The analyst who learned agent workflows in 2024 is not being replaced by an analyst AI today. Right? They're a strategic adviser who can do in a day what used to take a week. The entrepreneur who built their business on AR automation in 2025 is not scrambling to keep up. They're running a lean operation that would have required a team of 10 people 5 years ago. The window between this is new and most people don't know it and this is standard and everyone does it is getting shorter every cycle every year. Miniax M2.7 just dropped yesterday. It's self-improving. It's free on multiple platforms. It's competing with the best models in the world. In 6 months, models like this will be everywhere. The conversations that I'm having on this channel today will be the assumed baseline of knowledge in boardrooms and freelance contracts. The question isn't whether to get on board. The question is whether you get on board now or later because later um is more expensive than now. Not for example in resources but in time in the gap between where you could be and where you are. Let me tell you what I find most remarkable about this specific release. Not the benchmark scores, not the evolution capability. Not even the free access is the timeline. When compared to its predecessor M2.5 released in February 2026, the M2.7 model demonstrates significant gains in software engineering professional office tasks. February to March, one month, one month from the previous version to a version that jumped from midpack to top tier in global agent rankings. A version that participated in its own creation. A version that can build entire software projects, manage multi- aent teams, recover from production incidents in under 3 minutes, and do the work of a junior analyst. one month. That's not a normal pace of development. The model's evolution is further evidenced by a score of 50 on the artificial intelligence index representing an A point improvement. What does it do next month, the month after? Remember, Miniax is now using M2.7 to help build the next model. The self-improvement loop is already running. The next version is partially constructed by this version. I don't know what that means for the pace of improvement. Nobody does. We're in genuinely new territory. But I'll tell you what it means for the rest of us. It means the tools available to people who know how to use AI are going to be dramatically more powerful, dramatically more affordable, dramatically more capable in ways that are hard to predict even 6 months out. And the skills that transfer across all those improvements, knowing how to direct AI agents, knowing how to structure workflows, knowing how to evaluate AI output and iterate on it, those skills compound.
